hacker_news.title: Ask HN: Would this eliminate bots for good?
hacker_news.author: piratesAndSons
hacker_news.content_context:
I had an idea to eliminate the bot problem, or at the very least make it significantly harder to operate one. Here is my plan.

A new web browser built on a new HTTP protocol that accepts a human identity glove using cryptography. Instead of using your fingers directly on a mouse or trackpad, you wear a hardware glove that continuously records your pulse and your fingerprint, your machine information, and the average movement map that is unique to you as you interact with your device. The glove encrypts all of this information in real time. The browser then constantly verifies the glove hardware is present and active. No physical glove with a valid identity? No page loads.

What if someone tries to emulate the glove?

This is where the new browser becomes the second line of defense. It continuously checks the hardware signature and serial number of the glove. You can attempt to emulate it all you want, but the probability of simultaneously spoofing the correct fingerprint, a continuous and believable human pulse, a personalized movement map, and the exact hardware serial number is as close to impossible as any security system can get.

What do you all think of this as a prel... HN comment: Sell it to Mark Zuckerberg.

Smart Search – AI finds videos and channels instantly.

Voice Control – just speak or type what you want to watch.

Instant Streaming – fast, no lag.

Personalized Recommendations – learns your preferences over time.

Works on Smart TVs, mobile, and desktop.

We’re currently offering Basic to Premium packages (€4.99–€14.99/month) and a demo is available for early users.

I’d love feedback from the HN community on the AI recommendation engine and overall experience.

hacker_news.title: Vitest vs. Jest
hacker_news.author: howToTestFE
hacker_news.summary: Jest has served the JS community for years, but as of 2026 I strongly believe you should be only considering using Vitest going forward. Here are my reasons why!
comment_highlights: Blog post comparing Vitest vs Jest

linked_context.page_title: Vitest vs Jest: Why I would always pick Vitest over Jest in 2026 | How To Test Frontend
linked_context.page_description: Jest has served the JS community for years, but as of 2026 I strongly believe you should be only considering using Vitest going forward. Here are my reasons why!
linked_context.page_excerpt:
Jest dominated testing for a decade. But Vitest (released in late 2021) changed things, and as of 2026, I believe it's the better choice for new projects.

First released in 2014 (by Facebook), Jest was the clear dominant test runner for most of the last decade or so.

If you saw any JS test code, it was almost certainly going to be Jest. But then in late 2021/early 2022 Vitest was released and immediately everyone noticed how fast it was.

hacker_news.content_context:
Jest has served the JS community for years, but as of 2026 I strongly believe you should be only considering using Vitest going forward. Here are my reasons why!

Jest dominated testing for a decade. But Vitest (released in late 2021) changed things, and as of 2026, I believe it's the better choice for new projects. First released in 2014 (by Facebook), Jest was the clear dominant test runner for most of the last decade or so. If you saw any JS test code, it was almost certainly going to be Jest. But then in late 2021/early 2022 Vitest was released and immediately everyone noticed how fast it was.

hacker_news.title: The AI Powering Firefox's Shake to Summarize
hacker_news.author: bundie
hacker_news.summary: We recently released a feature in the Firefox iOS mobile app called “Shake to Summarize”. The reception was remarkably positive, earning an honorable m

linked_context.page_title: Under the hood: The AI powering Firefox’s Shake to Summarize | The Mozilla Blog
linked_context.page_description: We recently released a feature in the Firefox iOS mobile app called “Shake to Summarize”. The reception was remarkably positive, earning an honorable m
linked_context.page_excerpt:
We recently released a feature in the Firefox iOS mobile app called “ Shake to Summarize ”. The reception was remarkably positive, earning an honorable mention on Time Magazine’s best inventions of 2025 .

For anyone unfamiliar with Shake to Summarize, it’s just what the name implies: when you’re browsing a webpage, you can shake your phone to generate a short summary of the page’s content.

The gesture is fun, the feature is useful, and the whole thing feels simple and natural.

hacker_news.title: Why physical AI is becoming manufacturing's next advantage
hacker_news.author: Brajeshwar
hacker_news.summary: From simulation‑driven development to real‑world execution, Microsoft and NVIDIA are helping manufacturers leverage AI to cross the industrial frontier with confidence.

linked_context.page_title: Why physical AI is becoming manufacturing’s next advantage
linked_context.page_description: From simulation‑driven development to real‑world execution, Microsoft and NVIDIA are helping manufacturers leverage AI to cross the industrial frontier with confidence.
linked_context.page_excerpt:
From simulation‑driven development to real‑world execution, Microsoft and NVIDIA are helping manufacturers leverage AI to cross the industrial frontier with confidence.

For decades, manufacturers have pursued automation to drive efficiency, reduce costs, and stabilize operations. That approach delivered meaningful gains, but it is no longer enough.

Today’s manufacturing leaders face a different challenge: how to grow amid labor constraints, rising complexity, and increasing pressure to innovate faster without sacrificing safety, quality, or trust. The next phase of transformation will not be defined by isolated AI tools or individual robots, but by intelligence that can operate reliably in the physical world .

hacker_news.content_context:
From simulation‑driven development to real‑world execution, Microsoft and NVIDIA are helping manufacturers leverage AI to cross the industrial frontier with confidence.

From simulation‑driven development to real‑world execution, Microsoft and NVIDIA are helping manufacturers leverage AI to cross the industrial frontier with confidence. For decades, manufacturers have pursued automation to drive efficiency, reduce costs, and stabilize operations. That approach delivered meaningful gains, but it is no longer enough. Today’s manufacturing leaders face a different challenge: how to grow amid labor constraints, rising complexity, and increasing pressure to innovate faster without sacrificing safety, quality, or trust. The next phase of transformation will not be defined by isolated AI tools or individual robots, but by intelligence that can operate reliably in the physical world .

top_trends.title: Ask HN: Would this eliminate bots for good?
top_trends.author: piratesAndSons
top_trends.content_context:
I had an idea to eliminate the bot problem, or at the very least make it significantly harder to operate one. Here is my plan.

A new web browser built on a new HTTP protocol that accepts a human identity glove using cryptography. Instead of using your fingers directly on a mouse or trackpad, you wear a hardware glove that continuously records your pulse and your fingerprint, your machine information, and the average movement map that is unique to you as you interact with your device. The glove encrypts all of this information in real time. The browser then constantly verifies the glove hardware is present and active. No physical glove with a valid identity? No page loads.

What if someone tries to emulate the glove?

This is where the new browser becomes the second line of defense. It continuously checks the hardware signature and serial number of the glove. You can attempt to emulate it all you want, but the probability of simultaneously spoofing the correct fingerprint, a continuous and believable human pulse, a personalized movement map, and the exact hardware serial number is as close to impossible as any security system can get.

What do you all think of this as a prel... HN comment: Sell it to Mark Zuckerberg.

top_trends.content_context:
Hi HN,

I built FalconAI, an AI-powered streaming platform that lets you find your favorite shows instantly—no channel lists, no waiting.

Key features:

Smart Search – AI finds videos and channels instantly.

Voice Control – just speak or type what you want to watch.

Instant Streaming – fast, no lag.

Personalized Recommendations – learns your preferences over time.

Works on Smart TVs, mobile, and desktop.

We’re currently offering Basic to Premium packages (€4.99–€14.99/month) and a demo is available for early users.

I’d love feedback from the HN community on the AI recommendation engine and overall experience.
